ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general nasi lemak entry; the chicken wing and cutlet variant is not separately coded. Mapped to the closest parent concept.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for nasi lemak with chicken wing and cutlet. A0FKP (composite rice dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi lemak or this specific variant as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak with chicken wing and chicken cutlet in Singapore typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising coconut rice (~150 g), one fried chicken wing (~80 g), one fried chicken cutlet (~100 g), sambal (~30 g), ikan bilis (~15 g), peanuts (~10 g), cucumber slices (~10 g), and a hard-boiled egg (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (Chicken Wing and Chicken Cutlet) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Nasi Lemak (Chicken Wing and Chicken Cutlet), cr_id F05100086. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and coconut rice composite analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi lemak with chicken wing and cutlet in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: cooked white jasmine rice with coconut milk (GI ~68, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 82.24 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 55.9, rounded to 56.1 accounting for fibre adjustment. This is an analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 495 mg/100 g — well above the 400 mg/100 g threshold, driven by sambal, ikan bilis, and seasoned fried chicken; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,980 mg sodium, representing 86% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit this dish to small portions (≤150 g), request reduced sambal and ikan bilis, choose grilled rather than deep-fried chicken, and avoid adding soy sauce or additional condiments to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 20.56 g/100 g from white coconut rice (GI ~68, medium-high), with low dietary fibre (1.92 g/100 g) providing minimal glycaemic buffering; a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 82 g of available carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion size to ≤150 g of rice, substitute deep-fried chicken cutlet with grilled chicken to reduce trans fat and saturated fat that worsen insulin resistance, and pair the meal with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk and frying oil, estimated trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying the chicken cutlet, and high sodium (495 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid the deep-fried chicken cutlet (substitute grilled or steamed chicken), request coconut rice prepared with reduced coconut milk, limit portion to ≤150 g, and avoid ikan bilis or sambal additions to reduce combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; estimated trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk and frying oil are pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute the deep-fried chicken cutlet and wing with grilled or steamed chicken, request reduced-coconut-milk rice preparation, and monitor response to sambal chilli (nightshade) if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Chicken Wing and Chicken Cutlet is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian Malay dish consisting of fragrant white rice cooked in coconut milk and pandan leaves, served with a deep-fried chicken wing, a deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet, spicy sambal chilli paste, crispy ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, and a hard-boiled egg. The coconut rice is rich, slightly sweet, and aromatic, while the deep-fried chicken components provide a crispy, savoury contrast. The sambal adds heat and umami depth. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 864 kcal, 42.7 g protein, 41.9 g fat, and 82.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 495 mg/100 g (approximately 1,980 mg per plate), making this dish a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The medium GI (~68) from white coconut rice and the low dietary fibre (1.92 g/100 g) make blood glucose management challenging for type 2 diabetes patients. The deep-fried preparation generates trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) and AGEs, which are pro-inflammatory. This dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is commonly consumed as breakfast or lunch in Singapore hawker centres and food courts, and is considered a national dish of Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi lemak with chicken?
Is nasi lemak healthy for diabetics?
Can I eat nasi lemak if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100086) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white jasmine rice with coconut milk, GI ~68) and is clearly documented as such. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from the HPB record and published literature on deep-fried chicken and coconut milk preparations; these secondary fields are marked accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid fractionation, and moisture data for this specific nasi lemak variant (chicken wing and chicken cutlet) are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05100086 as of 2026-04-16; the HPB Foodo database does not publish these extended micronutrient fields for composite hawker dishes, and no equivalent breakdown was found in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific dish combination. Saturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the HPB record context and published values for coconut milk rice and deep-fried chicken; the HPB source does not separately itemise saturated fat for this entry. Trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on deep-frying methodology documented in peer-reviewed literature; not directly reported by HPB. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white jasmine rice cooked with coconut milk as the closest approved analogue; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish variant exists in the database as of 2026. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a dish containing chicken and anchovies; this tag has been removed. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore hawker centre portion sizes for nasi lemak with two chicken pieces; no standardised HPB portion weight was available for this specific variant.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name 'Nasi Lemak with Chicken Wing and Chicken Cutlet' is precise and unambiguous. All five mandatory Singaporean language names are present: English (food_name), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (216.0), protein_g (10.68), fat_g (10.47), carbohydrate_g (20.56) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database cr_id F05100086 with confidence 0.95. nutrient_source fully populated with title, organization, URL, and match type.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.92), sodium_mg (495.0), saturated_fat_g (3.8), sugar_g (1.97) all present as numeric values. saturated_fat_g is an estimate documented in notes. All values are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (68), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (56.1), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate (white jasmine rice with coconut milk). Estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03311069, nasi lemak) with honest gap note that the chicken variant is not separately coded. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible anchor for composite rice dish with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are all populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor with specific clinical data, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid Arthritis included as additional condition preserving input data.

All five mandatory Singaporean language names present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as a documented analogous estimate. All -1 fields documented in notes with named approved sources confirming unavailability. Ontology anchors are defensible with honest gap notes and correct match types.
